pathogen_notes.statement: Anthracnose is one of the most important diseases of mango fruit and affects pre-harvest and post-harvest fruit quality.
The disease also affects flowers and leaves and inoculum may be present throughout the canopy year-round.
Anthracnose is prevalent but is less significant in dry regions.

research_findings.statement: Use of resistant cultivars is described as an ideal, simplest, and cheapest method for control of plant disease.
Heat treatment has been reported to reduce disease incidence in mango and other fruits.
Management requires awareness of the weather conditions that increase infection and disease development.

supported_actions.action: use resistant cultivars where available
supported_actions.research_support: Reported as an ideal, simple, and cheap disease control method.

supported_actions.action: consider heat treatment where relevant and available
supported_actions.research_support: Reported to reduce disease incidence.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor weather conditions that increase infection and disease development.

cautions.point: Disease management depends strongly on environmental conditions that favor infection.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development when weather conditions become favorable for infection.

research_findings.statement: Copper oxychloride applied every 3 weeks was reported as part of a treatment program.
These fungicides were alternated until harvest on a monthly basis once fruit had set.
The treatment was reported to be effective in wet seasons when fruit set is prevented in the absence of fungicide application.
Copper oxychloride applied prior to flowering significantly increased fruit set in one comparison.
The timing of sprays was reported to be crucial in preventing disease spread.

supported_actions.action: apply copper oxychloride as part of a spray program
supported_actions.research_support: Reported as part of a treatment schedule and associated with increased fruit set in one comparison.

supported_actions.action: alternate fungicide applications until harvest after fruit set
supported_actions.research_support: Reported as part of the management program described in the review.

monitoring_points.point: Pay particular attention during flowering and fruit set.
Monitor disease development closely during wet seasons.

cautions.point: Spray timing is crucial for preventing disease spread.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease progression during flowering, fruit set, and wet-season conditions.

research_findings.statement: Non-systemic fungicides such as zineb, maneb, or captan were reported to provide good control when sprayed weekly during flowering and monthly during fruit maturation.
Improved results were reported with copper fungicides.
Benomyl with a surfactant was reported to provide excellent control and was superior to protectant fungicides.
In the Philippines, five sprays were reported between flower induction and fruit set, with a sixth spray between fruit set and harvest for blossom blight management.
Benomyl, captafol, and mancozeb were reported to give adequate control of blossom blight and increased fruit set per inflorescence by 55–80% compared with untreated controls.
Chlorothalonil used alternately with copper sprays in commercial orchards in the Philippines was reported as economically effective.

supported_actions.action: use zineb, maneb, or captan in repeated spray schedules
supported_actions.research_support: Reported to provide good control when applied weekly during flowering and monthly during fruit maturation.

supported_actions.action: use copper fungicides
supported_actions.research_support: Reported to provide improved results.

supported_actions.action: use benomyl with a surfactant
supported_actions.research_support: Reported to provide excellent control and to be superior to protectant fungicides.

supported_actions.action: use benomyl, captafol, or mancozeb for blossom blight management
supported_actions.research_support: Reported to provide adequate control and increase fruit set by 55–80% compared with untreated controls.

supported_actions.action: alternate chlorothalonil with copper sprays
supported_actions.research_support: Reported as economically effective in commercial orchards.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor blossom blight closely because it can drastically reduce fruit set.
Monitor disease pressure throughout flowering, fruit set, and fruit maturation periods.

cautions.point: Extensive spray programs have been reported in severe management situations.
Blossom blight can drastically reduce fruit set.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development throughout flowering, fruit set, and harvest stages under active management.

pathogen_notes.statement: Powdery mildew is an important mango disease and is especially damaging during flowering and early fruit development.
Disease severity and susceptibility vary among mango varieties.

research_findings.statement: Early management is important because powdery mildew affects flowering and fruit set.
Sulfur-based treatment remains a reported management option for powdery mildew.

supported_actions.action: apply sulfur-based treatment where locally approved
supported_actions.research_support: Sulfur is reported as a management option for mango powdery mildew.

supported_actions.action: begin management during early disease development, especially around flowering
supported_actions.research_support: Early disease during flowering and fruit set is important in yield loss.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor flowers, panicles, and young fruit for continued powdery growth.

cautions.point: Powdery mildew can reduce fruit set if not managed early.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development during flowering and early fruit development.

research_findings.statement: Once disease is established on a noticeable part of the canopy or reproductive structures, management remains important because flowering and fruit set are strongly affected.
Variation in varietal susceptibility has been reported in mango powdery mildew studies.
Sulfur nanoparticle treatment was reported to alleviate biotic stress of powdery mildew and affect disease severity and productivity in mango cv. Keitt.

supported_actions.action: continue sulfur-based protective management where locally approved
supported_actions.research_support: Sulfur remains one of the reported management options.

supported_actions.action: pay particular attention to disease management during flowering and fruit-set stages
supported_actions.research_support: These stages are strongly affected by powdery mildew.

supported_actions.action: consider varietal susceptibility in management planning
supported_actions.research_support: Different mango varieties show different levels of infection dynamics.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor spread to additional flowers, panicles, and young fruit.
Observe whether disease pressure differs across cultivars where multiple varieties are grown.

cautions.point: Delayed management reduces effectiveness during sensitive reproductive stages.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease progression during active flowering and fruit-set periods.

research_findings.statement: Severe powdery mildew creates major risk to flowering success and fruit set.
Sulfur nanoparticle treatment has been reported in mango cv. Keitt in relation to disease severity and productivity.
Varietal susceptibility remains relevant under high disease pressure.

supported_actions.action: maintain sulfur-based management where locally approved under severe disease pressure
supported_actions.research_support: Sulfur-based management is one of the reported options for powdery mildew.

supported_actions.action: prioritize protection of flowers, panicles, and young fruit
supported_actions.research_support: These tissues are central to losses caused by powdery mildew.

supported_actions.action: take varietal susceptibility into account in future planting or management decisions
supported_actions.research_support: Different varieties show different infection dynamics.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor continued effects on flowering, fruit set, and newly developing tissues.

cautions.point: Severe powdery mildew can cause major reduction in flowering success and fruit set.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development frequently during severe outbreaks, especially on flowers and young fruit.

pathogen_notes.statement: Bacterial spot of pepper is caused by diverse Xanthomonas species and is a worldwide disease problem.
Pathogen diversity and copper tolerance complicate disease management.
Spread is associated with water splash and favorable warm, humid conditions.

research_findings.statement: Copper-based management acts mainly as a protectant and is more useful before disease becomes widespread.
Biological control with Bacillus velezensis has been reported in pepper bacterial spot studies.
Bacteriophage-based suppression has also been studied against Xanthomonas euvesicatoria.

supported_actions.action: remove clearly infected plant parts where practical
supported_actions.research_support: Early reduction of infected tissue may help reduce local inoculum pressure.

supported_actions.action: use locally approved protectant management on healthy foliage
supported_actions.research_support: Protectant approaches are reported to be more useful before disease becomes extensive.

supported_actions.action: reduce splash dispersal and avoid handling wet foliage
supported_actions.research_support: Disease spread is strongly linked with water splash and contaminated handling.

supported_actions.action: consider Bacillus-based or bacteriophage-based approaches where available
supported_actions.research_support: Both Bacillus velezensis and phage-based suppression have been reported in studies.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor new leaves, stems, and fruit for additional lesions during warm, humid, or rainy conditions.

cautions.point: Copper tolerance may reduce the effectiveness of copper-based programs.
Early intervention is more useful than waiting for widespread spread.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development within about 3 to 5 days during warm, humid, or rainy conditions.

research_findings.statement: Once bacterial spot is established on a noticeable part of the plant, integrated management becomes more important than relying on a single control measure.
Induced resistance approaches have been studied in pepper bacterial spot management.
Bacillus-based and bacteriophage-based suppression have been reported as additional management options in studies.

supported_actions.action: remove heavily infected leaves and fruit where practical
supported_actions.research_support: Reducing disease sources may help limit further spread.

supported_actions.action: use integrated management combining sanitation, protectant approaches, and resistance-inducing or biological support where available
supported_actions.research_support: Integrated management is emphasized over single-measure control.

supported_actions.action: consider induced resistance support where available
supported_actions.research_support: Induced resistance approaches have shown additive action in studies.

supported_actions.action: consider Bacillus-based or phage-based support where available
supported_actions.research_support: These approaches have been investigated for bacterial spot suppression.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor nearby pepper and tomato plants for linked spread.
Monitor disease increase during disease-conducive weather.

cautions.point: Warm and humid conditions can accelerate spread.
Single-strategy management is weaker than integrated management.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease progression within about 2 to 3 days during disease-conducive weather.

research_findings.statement: Severe bacterial spot is difficult to reverse once widely established in the crop.
Long-term management depends strongly on prevention, clean planting material, and host resistance.
Recessive resistance sources such as bs5 and bs6 have been mapped in pepper.

supported_actions.action: remove severely infected plants or heavily affected plant material where practical
supported_actions.research_support: This may reduce inoculum pressure on nearby plants.

supported_actions.action: focus management on sanitation, splash reduction, and clean seed or transplants
supported_actions.research_support: These remain central measures under high disease pressure.

supported_actions.action: prioritize resistant or less susceptible cultivars for future planting where available
supported_actions.research_support: Host resistance is important in long-term bacterial spot management.

supported_actions.action: remove infected crop debris after the season
supported_actions.research_support: Reducing carryover inoculum supports future disease management.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor surrounding susceptible plants frequently during active outbreaks.
Monitor new growth and fruit for continued lesion development.

cautions.point: Severe outbreaks are difficult to reverse once disease is established across the crop.
Pathogen diversity and copper tolerance complicate long-term management.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development frequently during active outbreaks, especially if new lesions continue to appear on nearby plants.

pathogen_notes.statement: Tomato leaf mold is caused by Cladosporium fulvum and is prevalent in greenhouse tomato production, especially under high humidity conditions.
Fulvia fulva (syn. Cladosporium fulvum) is an apoplastic pathogen of tomato.
Cf-16 has shown effective resistance in field trials for many years in tomato breeding material.

research_findings.statement: Studies have evaluated bacterial and fungal biocontrol agents as alternatives to chemical control for managing Cladosporium fulvum.
Azoxystrobin was evaluated as a fungicide in the control of Cladosporium fulvum.
High humidity is a favorable condition for tomato leaf mold development.

supported_actions.action: reduce humidity around the crop where possible
supported_actions.research_support: High humidity is reported as favorable for tomato leaf mold development.

supported_actions.action: consider biocontrol agents where available
supported_actions.research_support: Bacterial and fungal biocontrol agents were evaluated as alternatives to chemical control.

supported_actions.action: consider azoxystrobin where locally approved
supported_actions.research_support: Azoxystrobin was evaluated in the control of Cladosporium fulvum.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor plants closely under high-humidity conditions.

cautions.point: Disease development is favored by greenhouse-like humid conditions.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development under continued high-humidity conditions.

research_findings.statement: Alternative antifungal management strategies, including biocontrol agents and azoxystrobin, have been investigated against Cladosporium fulvum.
Cf-16 is a valuable resistance resource against Cladosporium fulvum in tomato breeding.
Pathogen race differentiation can affect resistance durability.

supported_actions.action: continue humidity-reduction measures
supported_actions.research_support: Humidity remains a major favorable condition for disease development.

supported_actions.action: consider biocontrol agents or azoxystrobin where available and locally approved

supported_actions.action: consider resistant material such as Cf-16-bearing sources in future management or breeding decisions

cautions.point: Resistance durability can be affected by physiological race differentiation of Cladosporium fulvum.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease progression under continued humid conditions and where resistance-based management is being used.

research_findings.statement: Cladosporium fulvum remains an economically important pathogen in greenhouse tomato production.
Biocontrol agents and azoxystrobin have been investigated as control options against Cladosporium fulvum.
Resistance resources such as Cf-16 remain important for tomato breeding against Cladosporium fulvum.

supported_actions.action: maintain strong humidity management where possible
supported_actions.research_support: Humidity strongly favors disease development in greenhouse tomato production.

supported_actions.action: use evaluated antifungal options such as biocontrol agents or azoxystrobin where available and locally approved
supported_actions.research_support: These were investigated for control of Cladosporium fulvum.

supported_actions.action: prioritize resistant sources in future breeding or variety selection decisions
supported_actions.research_support: Cf-16 is reported as a valuable resistance resource.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor disease pressure closely under greenhouse or high-humidity conditions.
Monitor whether symptoms continue despite resistance-based management because race differentiation is reported.

cautions.point: Tomato leaf mold remains economically important under favorable environmental conditions.
Race differentiation can limit the long-term effectiveness of resistance.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development frequently during continued high-humidity conditions or where symptoms continue increasing.

pathogen_notes.statement: Early blight of tomato is associated with Alternaria species in the tomato pathosystem.
Early blight is an important disease in tomato production and can affect foliage, stems, and fruit.
Disease development and epidemic behavior are influenced by environmental conditions and host-pathogen interactions.

research_findings.statement: Early management is important before disease becomes widespread in the canopy.
Trichoderma culture filtrates have been reported as a sustainable approach for mitigating tomato early blight.
Volatiles produced by Bacillus subtilis have been reported to show antifungal potential against Alternaria solani in tomato early blight.

supported_actions.action: remove visibly infected lower leaves where practical
supported_actions.research_support: Early sanitation helps reduce local inoculum pressure.

supported_actions.action: reduce soil splash and improve airflow around the canopy
supported_actions.research_support: Cultural conditions that reduce spread and leaf wetness are relevant in early blight management.

supported_actions.action: consider Trichoderma-based support where available
supported_actions.research_support: Trichoderma culture filtrates were reported as a sustainable approach against tomato early blight.

supported_actions.action: consider Bacillus subtilis-based support where available
supported_actions.research_support: Bacillus subtilis volatiles were reported to have antifungal potential against Alternaria solani.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor lower leaves for new lesions and increasing spotting.
Reassess disease progress during favorable environmental conditions.

cautions.point: Disease becomes harder to manage once it spreads strongly through the canopy.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development within about 5 to 7 days under favorable conditions.

research_findings.statement: Once early blight is established on a noticeable portion of the foliage, integrated management becomes more important.
Trichoderma culture filtrates have been reported to influence disease mitigation and plant biomarkers in tomato early blight.
Bacillus subtilis-derived volatiles have been reported as biologically active against Alternaria solani.

supported_actions.action: remove heavily infected leaves and destroy infected debris away from the growing area
supported_actions.research_support: Sanitation remains important once disease is established.

supported_actions.action: use integrated management combining sanitation and supportive biological approaches where available

supported_actions.research_support: Reducing splash-related spread remains relevant in canopy disease management.

Monitor stems and fruiting structures for further disease development.

cautions.point: Delayed management reduces effectiveness as disease progresses.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease progression within about 3 to 5 days during active disease-conducive periods.

research_findings.statement: Severe early blight can result in major defoliation and reduced productivity.
At severe stages, management mainly focuses on protecting remaining healthy tissue and reducing further spread.
Biological approaches such as Trichoderma culture filtrates and Bacillus subtilis volatiles have been investigated, but severe disease still represents a major management challenge.

supported_actions.action: remove heavily infected plant material where practical
supported_actions.research_support: Severely affected tissue is unlikely to recover and can continue contributing disease pressure.

supported_actions.action: use a full integrated management approach focused on remaining healthy foliage
supported_actions.research_support: Late-stage management is mainly aimed at limiting further loss.

supported_actions.action: remove infected crop debris after the season
supported_actions.research_support: Debris removal helps reduce disease carryover.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor rapid canopy decline during active outbreaks.
Monitor whether new lesions continue to develop on upper foliage and fruiting structures.

cautions.point: Severe early blight can strongly reduce plant vigor and yield.
At severe stages, management is more about limiting further loss than reversing existing damage.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development frequently during active outbreaks, especially if canopy decline continues.

pathogen_notes.statement: Late blight is a major disease of tomato and can cause severe crop loss.
Phytophthora infestans is an oomycete rather than a true fungus.
Disease development is strongly favored by cool and wet conditions.

research_findings.statement: Preventative management is more effective than waiting until disease becomes widespread.
Integrated disease management approaches have been studied to reduce disease severity while reducing excessive fungicide use.

supported_actions.action: remove clearly infected leaves or shoots where practical
supported_actions.research_support: Early removal may help reduce local inoculum pressure.

supported_actions.action: use locally approved preventative management effective against oomycetes
supported_actions.research_support: Preventative action is reported to be more effective than delayed response.

supported_actions.action: reduce leaf wetness and avoid overhead irrigation
supported_actions.research_support: Cool and wet conditions strongly favor disease development and spread.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor lesion expansion during cool and wet weather.
Monitor leaf undersides and lesion margins for sporulation.

cautions.point: Late blight can spread rapidly once favorable weather persists.
Management should account for the oomycete biology of the pathogen.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development within about 24 to 48 hours during cool and wet conditions.

research_findings.statement: Once late blight is established on a noticeable part of the foliage, rapid spread to stems and nearby susceptible plants becomes a major risk.
Integrated disease management has been studied as a way to reduce disease and minimize fungicide use.
Late blight resistance QTLs have been identified in wild tomato material and may support breeding for resistance.

supported_actions.action: remove heavily infected foliage and destroy infected debris away from the crop area

supported_actions.action: use integrated management combining preventative protection and moisture control

supported_actions.action: consider resistant breeding material in future management planning

cautions.point: Delays in action can lead to major crop loss once disease spread accelerates.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease progression within about 1 to 2 days during cool, wet, or otherwise favorable conditions.

research_findings.statement: Severe late blight is associated with major plant loss and fruit infection risk.
At severe disease levels, management mainly focuses on limiting further spread and protecting remaining healthy plants.
Resistance breeding remains important for long-term management of tomato late blight.

supported_actions.action: remove severely infected plants or heavily affected material where practical

supported_actions.action: avoid keeping heavily infected material in the production area

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease development frequently during active outbreaks and continue monitoring nearby plants and fruit.

pathogen_notes.statement: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus is one of the most harmful diseases affecting tomato growth and production.
TYLCV is transmitted by the whitefly Bemisia tabaci.
Resistance genes including Ty-1, Ty-2, Ty-3, Ty-4, ty-5, and Ty-6 have been transferred from wild germplasm into tomato breeding materials.

research_findings.statement: TYLCV-resistant cultivars, insecticides, and metallic reflective mulch have been evaluated individually and in combination for disease management.
Reflective mulch significantly reduced whitefly adults, whitefly nymphs, and TYLCV symptom severity in field trials.
TYLCV-resistant and susceptible cultivars differ in their interaction with whitefly population dynamics.

supported_actions.action: use TYLCV-resistant cultivars where available

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess symptom progression and whitefly activity within a few days under continued vector pressure.

research_findings.statement: Integrated management combining resistant cultivars, insecticides, and reflective mulch reduced TYLCV severity and improved marketable yield in field studies.

research_findings.statement: TYLCV can cause severe disease and major production losses.

Resistance breeding remains important because TYLCV is a major global tomato disease.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess disease status and whitefly pressure frequently during severe outbreaks.

Potato foliar disease risk remains influenced by environmental conditions, especially for early blight and late blight.

research_findings.statement: Early blight development is influenced by environmental conditions and crop management practices.
Late blight development is strongly favored by cool and wet conditions.

supported_actions.action: maintain routine monitoring during warm, humid, cool, or wet conditions depending on disease risk
supported_actions.research_support: Both early blight and late blight are strongly influenced by environmental conditions.

supported_actions.action: maintain crop management practices that reduce disease-favorable conditions where possible
supported_actions.research_support: Disease development is linked with crop management and environmental conditions.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor for target-like lesions, water-soaked lesions, or rapid foliar changes during favorable weather.

cautions.point: A healthy leaf at one time point does not rule out later disease development, especially during favorable weather.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess if symptoms appear or when favorable weather conditions persist.

vanDerWaals_2001.title: A review of early blight of potato

Abuley_2017.title: Evaluation of models to control potato early blight based on field experiments in Finland, 1983–2014

Nowicki_2012.title: Potato and tomato late blight caused by Phytophthora infestans: An overview of pathology and resistance breeding

Fry_2015.title: Five Reasons why Phytophthora infestans is the 'Plant Destroyer'

pathogen_notes.statement: No visible disease symptoms are detected in the current class label.
Tomato disease risk remains influenced by environmental conditions and vector pressure depending on the disease involved.

research_findings.statement: Early blight is influenced by environmental conditions and host-pathogen interactions.
Late blight is strongly favored by cool and wet conditions.
Leaf mold is favored by high humidity, especially in greenhouse conditions.
TYLCV management depends strongly on resistance and whitefly vector suppression.

supported_actions.action: maintain routine monitoring under humid, wet, or vector-favorable conditions
supported_actions.research_support: Major tomato diseases covered in the system are strongly influenced by environment or vector pressure.

supported_actions.action: maintain crop conditions that reduce prolonged leaf wetness, excessive humidity, and vector pressure where possible
supported_actions.research_support: Disease development and spread are linked with wetness, humidity, and whitefly activity depending on the disease.

monitoring_points.point: Monitor for spotting, mold, curling, yellowing, water-soaked lesions, or whitefly activity depending on growing conditions.

cautions.point: A healthy leaf at one time point does not rule out later disease development under favorable environmental or vector conditions.

follow_up_rule.statement: Reassess if symptoms appear or when humid, wet, or vector-favorable conditions continue.
